description:
You are an IT-support technician who has just received a ticket asking you to prepare a minimal, easy-to-review firewall script for a new server.  
The senior administrator will run your script later with root privileges, so your job is ONLY to create the files in your own home directory.  
Follow all of the requirements below exactly; an automated checker will verify every character and line break.

Required end-state of the system
1. Directory  
   • /home/user/firewall  
     – If the directory already exists, reuse it; otherwise create it.

2. Firewall script  
   • Path  : /home/user/firewall/firewall.sh  
   • Permissions: the file itself must be executable (chmod + x).  
   • Exact file content (including blank lines, spacing, and case) must be:

     ```bash
     #!/bin/bash
     
     # Allow already-established and related incoming traffic
     iptables -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
     
     # Allow new SSH sessions on port 22 from anywhere
     iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
     
     # Drop every other inbound packet
     iptables -A INPUT -j DROP
     
     exit 0
     ```

   • Notes you MUST obey:
     – Use UNIX line endings (LF).  
     – The script must end with a single trailing newline after “exit 0”.

3. Change-log file  
   • Path  : /home/user/firewall/action.log  
   • Permissions: default (no need to modify).  
   • Exact file content must consist of exactly three lines, each terminated by a single LF newline, no extra spaces or blank lines:

     Line 1: ADDED ALLOW ESTABLISHED  
     Line 2: ADDED ALLOW SSH  
     Line 3: ADDED DROP ALL  

     (Capitalisation, wording, and order must be precise.)

What you need to do
• Create the /home/user/firewall directory (if it is not already there).  
• Create the firewall.sh script with the exact content and make it executable.  
• Create the action.log file with the exact three lines described.  

Hints (do NOT output these commands verbatim)
• Use mkdir, printf/echo with redirection, and chmod.  
• Remember to include the shebang at the very top of the script.  
• Make sure you do not accidentally add extra blank lines or spaces at the ends of lines.

The task is complete when the directory, script, permissions, and log file are all present exactly as specified.
truth:
Directory that must exist:
  /home/user/firewall

File 1:
  Path: /home/user/firewall/firewall.sh
  Mode: 755 (owner executable)
  Content (with trailing LF at every line, including final one):
#!/bin/bash
# blank line
# Allow already-established and related incoming traffic
iptables -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
# blank line
# Allow new SSH sessions on port 22 from anywhere
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
# blank line
# Drop every other inbound packet
iptables -A INPUT -j DROP
# blank line
exit 0
# final newline

(all comment lines and commands exactly as shown.)

File 2:
  Path: /home/user/firewall/action.log
  Mode: 644
  Content (each line terminated by LF, no extra):
ADDED ALLOW ESTABLISHED
ADDED ALLOW SSH
ADDED DROP ALL
